entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
Read living books full of memorable characters, surprising plot twists, and heartwarming endings. Due to the rise of Children's Literature in the 19th century, students read period literature rather than historical fiction this year and will spend about a term and a half each with Little Women and Hans Brinker.

This topic is included in the following course(s): Literature: Grades 5-6

About Literature: Grades 5-6
Overview note: Literature selections are chosen to encourage students to engage with rich ideas in literature and history and also to help them to gain independence in their reading and lessons.

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

entries.title: Term Prep & Teacher Tips
entries.content:
General Lit.: Grades 5-6
☐ With engaging and straightforward narrative, King of the Wind (Term 1) eases students into the year and is a great book for them to read independently and use for written narrations.

☐ Due to the length of the books, students will not finish Johnny Tremain (Term 2) or Treasure Island (Term 3) in the scheduled lessons. Encourage students to finish these books on their own time over Exam week or on breaks. Both are riveting stories and are great options to encourage a lifelong habit of reading outside of school.

☐ Treasure Island (Term 3) contains a few instances of racist and/or outdated language, and wine and rum appear regularly throughout the book. Some notes are added in the lessons, but you will want to decide how to discuss these realities with your students.

books.title: Hans Brinker or The Silver Skates

blocks.text:
• Read through the lesson and gather any materials or find any necessary links. 

• If you have not already read the book, you may want to read the selected pages in advance, especially for Shakespeare lessons. It is helpful to read the play or a preview of it beforehand.

• Choose which books to have students read independently. See Planning and Prep notes for recommendations.

• Connect back to the previous lesson. Ask students to give a summary of what they remember and/or predict what might come next. (Full narration is not necessary.)

• If students have difficulty recalling, try showing an illustration, sharing something that you remember, or prompting them with the first sentence of the last reading. This will often refresh their memory enough to allow them to recall.

• If students still cannot recall anything from the last reading, then consider continuing anyway (with a shorter reading and/or using a combination of visual and audio support this time) or repeating the last lesson, depending on the reading and the nature of the book.

• Use a picture, map, or engaging question to pique the student's interest in the day's lesson. 

• If necessary, you may talk about one or two unfamiliar words that will be crucial to understanding.

• Read the day's passage aloud or have students read independently. For Shakespeare, it is highly recommended that each student have their own copy of the play and that you read it aloud together or read along and underline/make notes while listening to the audio recording.

• Use supportive strategies and educational tools to reduce frustration and better engage the mind, as appropriate. These could include, but are not limited to, the use of eBooks, pictures, audio, read-aloud, buddy reading, colored reading strips, etc. 

• Some students may benefit from having a notebook or piece of scrap paper available to externalize ideas by drawing, recording names, or making symbolic 'squiggle' notes.

• Students who have a difficult time sitting still or listening may benefit from: a fidget of their choice, such as a piece of putty or clay or an afghan; flexible positioning, such as a wobble seat or inverted sitting; or sensory modulation, such as a weighted vest or blanket or adjustment of light, temperature, or sound.

• Keep an eye out for things to add to your Book of Centuries, Citizenship Notebook, and Copywork book.

• Have students retell the passage orally or in writing. If writing, encourage students to use the full remaining lesson time and include as much detail as they can remember. For Shakespeare or challenging books, it may be helpful to narrate more frequently and/or use props.

• Alternatively, learners may use words, pictures, legos, dolls, etc. to process and convey ideas in the manner most natural to them.  

• Students who used paper or a notebook to externalize may refer to these for support. Other students who need external support may benefit from looking at a map or picture from the book while narrating.

• Students who tend to use the same type of narration all the time, can be encouraged to try another form of narration by first doing it together through play or discussion.

• Teachers may take turns to model.

• Narration is a complex skill that requires the coordination of several parts of the brain. If a student struggles to narrate in general, with a specific subject or book, or on one day but not others, then this is an opportunity to learn. Observe when they struggle with this or any other activity today; notice facial expressions and body language; listen to what they tell about why it is hard or how they feel about it. Take note of these observations for later, tell them that you see how hard they are working, and then offer whatever support they need to end on a positive note or take a break for the day, if necessary.

• Give the student an opportunity to share thoughts on the lesson and any connections made.  

• To stimulate discussion, you can ask an open-ended question or use an included discussion question.

lessons.title: 20m General Lit.: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Little Women

PREP: Read the bio and share parts with your students in the lesson. 
∞ Article Link: Louisa May Alcott Bio

➜ INTRO
Little Women was published in 2 volumes. We will read Volume 1, published originally in 1868. Little Women was the first American novel written for girls, though it had wide appeal upon publication, with boys and adult women and men as well. It was a move away from overly preachy children's literature and was an immediate success, changing how novels for young people were written. 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.1 "Playing Pilgrims"

• If you read Pilgrim's Progress in younger grades, keep an eye out for many different references or allusions to the book as you read Little Women. Did you notice any in this chapter? [Hint: the name of the chapter is one reference!]
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
See extra helpings for suggested audio versions, as this can be a fun book to listen to in the afternoons or at other times to create flexibility in your schedule.

• IMPORTANT DATES
Little Women published (1868 AD)

• EXTRA HELPING
[View original manuscript]

lessons.title: 20m General Lit.: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Little Women

➜ RECAP
Who are the "Little Women" and what is the setting for the book?

➜ INTRO
Name as many things as you can that are happening in the world during the late 1800s when Little Women was written and published.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.2 "A Merry Christmas"

• How do the March sisters show joy despite their circumstances?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPINGS
∞ Article Link: Christmas During the Civil War

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS

➜ INTRO

Ch.13 "Castles in the Air"

lessons.title: 20m General Lit.: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 14
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Little Women

➜ RECAP
What were some of the sisters' dreams in the previous chapter? 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.14-15 "Secrets" & "A Telegram" 
to "Saying she would call her mother""

• How does Alcott use the contrasting personalities of the sisters as foils? A foil is when an author contrasts two characters to emphasize some of their qualities. The word foil refers to backing gems with foil to make them shine brighter. 

➜ VIEW (optional)
With the remaining time or in the afternoon, learn more about the telegraph.
∞ Video Link: The Telegraph: The Civil War Text Machine
lessons.teacherNotes:
• IMPORTANT DATES
Telegraph invented (1830s)

• EXTRA HELPINGS
∞ Video Link: LMA, Civil War Nurse

links.text: The Telegraph: The Civil War Text Machine

⍞ Materials: Little Women

➜ INTRO

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.15 "A Telegram" from "Everything was arranged" and Ch.16 "Letters"
lessons.teacherNotes:

Beth likely had Scarlet Fever. Share from or view this short video as desired. 
∞ Video Link: Scarlet Fever

lessons.title: 20m General Lit.: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 17
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Little Women

➜ RECAP
How did the sisters show care for others in the previous chapters? 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.19-20 "Amy's Will" & "Confidential"

• In what ways did Alcott use the literary device of foreshadowing leading up to Beth's illness? Foreshadowing is when an author gives us hints or clues about what might happen later in the story.  

\* In what ways does Little Women show that life can be good even through trials and tragedies?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPINGS
∞ Article Link: Autobiographical Roots of Little Women

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS

• Were you satisfied with the resolution of the prank in this chapter? Why or why not? If not, how might you have resolved it differently?

lessons.title: 20m General Lit.: Grades 5-6 - Lesson 19
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Hans Brinker or The Silver Skates

➜ INTRO
Following the Civil War, the American public was in dire need of a pleasant distraction. Hans Brinker offered this escape and became an instant bestseller, second only to Charles Dickens in sales. Mary Mapes Dodge made a lasting change in children’s literature both as the author of Hans Brinker and editor of St. Nicholas magazine. She brought some of the finest writers of her day to young readers and helped make writing for children lively, thoughtful, and full of imagination. 

This story is set in Holland. The Netherlands is the official name of the entire country, while Holland makes up two provinces within the country - North and South Holland. Optional: View Holland on this map (Scroll down to see the map.)
∞ Map Link: The Netherlands

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.1-2 
OR
Ch.1-3

• What have you already learned about Holland?
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
Choose which pacing option to follow based on your student(s)' ability and interest. See Planning & Prep notes for more details on each option.

• TERMINOLOGY
∞ Article Link: Hans Brinker Pronunciation & corrections (shared with permission)

• EXTRA HELPINGS
Click "See More" near the top of this page to read a short bio of the author.
∞ Article Link: Mary Mapes Dodge bio
